2 min read
Just now

--

Day 105 of my game dev journey — beginner lessons, real confusion, real progress.
I thought learning Unity would be easy after spending months in Unreal Engine.
Instead, even simple tasks felt strangely difficult.
That’s when it hit me: I wasn’t learning a new engine. I was trying to force Unreal Engine habits into Unity.
This post is part of my daily learning journey in game development, where I share the basics, the confusion, and the real progress — from the perspective of a beginner.
On Day 105, I explored what it actually feels like to switch from Unreal Engine to Unity.
What I Used to Think
Since I already knew Unreal Engine, I assumed Unity would come naturally. Both engines build games, after all. I expected my existing knowledge to transfer almost instantly.
It didn’t — not the way I expected.
What I Realized
The concepts are similar. The workflow isn’t.
Unity is built around GameObjects and Components. Unreal Engine is built around Actors, Components, and Blueprints. Same destination, different roads.
Even basic tasks — creating objects, navigating the editor, finding settings — felt unfamiliar, because I kept expecting Unreal’s layout and logic to show up in Unity.
Why This Was Harder Than Expected
The real challenge wasn’t relearning game development. It was unlearning how I think about engines.
I wasn’t struggling with Unity. I was struggling with my Unreal habits. Every time I searched for an “Unreal equivalent” instead of learning Unity’s own approach, I slowed myself down.
What Finally Clicked
The problem wasn’t Unity. The problem was my mindset.
Once I stopped comparing every feature to Unreal and started learning Unity on its own terms, things got easier — fast.
Instead of asking “Where’s the Unreal version of this?” I started asking “How does Unity solve this problem?”
That one shift changed everything.
How to Make the Switch Easier
Learn Unity’s terminology before comparing it to your old engine. Build one small project start to finish instead of just browsing the editor. Accept that workflows differ, even when concepts overlap. Focus on one engine at a time while you’re learning. And expect menus, tools, and shortcuts to live in completely different places than you’re used to.
Why This Matters Beyond Unity
Professional developers often switch between engines throughout their careers. The valuable skill was never memorizing editor layouts — it’s understanding game development fundamentals and adapting that knowledge to whatever workflow you’re in.
Once I stopped treating Unity like “a different version of Unreal” and started treating it as its own engine, learning got genuinely enjoyable.
The Takeaway
Switching engines doesn’t mean starting from zero. You already have the fundamentals. It’s just about learning a new way to apply them.
Slow progress — but a strong foundation.
If you’ve switched engines before, what was the first thing that confused you? Drop it below 👇
See you in the next post 🎮🚀
